coredata.dc:title: Phase stability and site preference of the disordered TbCu7-type compound PrCo7−x T x (T=Ti, Zr, Hf, Cu)
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Gang
Chen, Nan-Xian
Shen, Jiang
coredata.dc:description: The phase stability, lattice constants and site preference of the disordered TbCu7-type rare-earth intermetallic compound PrCo7−x T x (T=Ti, Zr, Hf, Cu) are studied via interatomic pair potentials based on Chen's lattice inversion method. The results show that the ternary element T (T=Ti, Zr, Hf) can decrease the internal energy and stabilize the PrCo7 structure. The calculations also show that the T atoms preferentially occupy the 2e sites. The calculated lattice parameters and site preference are in agreement with the experimental data. The results indicate that the interatomic pair potentials are valid for studying some structural properties of the disordered intermetallics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phase stability
Site preference
Interatomic potential
TbCu7-type

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The process of evolution from ordered PrCo5 to disordered PrCo7
Phase stability and site preference of PrCo7−x T x
Molecular dynamic calculation for PrCo7−x Zr x

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Potentials of Pr–Co–T (T=Ti, Zr, Hf, Cu).

(1–4) The process of evolution from the PrCo5 structure to PrCo7 with the structure TbCu7. (5) The calculated and experimental XRD patterns of PrCo7. *To show the process more clearly, only the Pr atoms and the corresponding dumbbell Co atoms are visible in (2)–(4).
Dependence of the PrCo7−x T x cohesive energy on the content of T (Ti, Zr, Hf, Cu).
The lattice constants and volume variation with the ternary element x in PrCo7−x T x .
a1–c1 are the structures of PrCo5; a2–c2 are the corresponding deformed structures of PrCo7. “o” at c2 represents the 2e site atoms nearby.
The parameters of some important potentials
Final crystal lattice constants of PrCo7 corresponding to random initial structures
Comparison between the calculated and experimental lattice parameters of PrCo7−x T x
The comparison of related lattice constants before and after atomic random motion of 0.5Å for PrCo7−x Zr x
Determination of the lattice constants of PrCo6.7Zr0.3
The lattice constants, potential energy and kinetic energy of PrCo6.86Zr0.14 and PrCo6.7Zr0.3 at different temperature

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Shown in parentheses are the energy fluctuations at difference temperatures.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Benefit factors for distinct Co sites

article.ce:title: Phase stability and site preference of the disordered TbCu7-type compound PrCo7−x T x (T=Ti, Zr, Hf, Cu)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Applied Physics, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The phase stability, lattice constants and site preference of the disordered TbCu7-type rare-earth intermetallic compound PrCo7−x T x (T=Ti, Zr, Hf, Cu) are studied via interatomic pair potentials based on Chen's lattice inversion method. The results show that the ternary element T (T=Ti, Zr, Hf) can decrease the internal energy and stabilize the PrCo7 structure. The calculations also show that the T atoms preferentially occupy the 2e sites. The calculated lattice parameters and site preference are in agreement with the experimental data. The results indicate that the interatomic pair potentials are valid for studying some structural properties of the disordered intermetallics.

ce:section.ce:para: Rare-earth–cobalt (R–Co) compounds with TbCu7-type structure have attracted much attention recently during the effort to find new magnets or improve existing magnets particularly for high-temperature applications [1–3]. The TbCu7-type structure is a derivative of the CaCu5-type structure (space group P6/mmm) and can be visualized as TbCu5-based compounds in which 2/9 of the Tb sites are randomly substituted by ‘dumbbell’ pairs of Cu–Cu. A pure binary RCo7 compound with the TbCu7-type structure does not exist at room temperature, and a third metal element is necessary in order to stabilize the R–Co 1:7 phase with the TbCu7-type structure. It has been known that Zr, Cu, Ti and Mn can stabilize the SmCo7 phase and Zr and Hf can stabilize the PrCo7 phase. PrCo7 phase cannot be stabilized by Cu and Ti, and the single PrCo7−x Ti x can only be formed by mechanical milling [1,2,4–9].

In the previous work of our group, the interatomic pair potentials obtained by Chen's lattice inversion method [10,11] have been successfully used in the process of evolution from RT5 structure (CaCu5-type) to ordered compounds RT12 (ThMn12-type), R2T17 (Th2Zn17-type and Th2Ni17-type) and R3T29 (Nd3Fe29−x Ti x -type) [12–14]. In this paper, the pair potentials are used to simulate the evolution from RT5 to disordered compound RT7 (TbCu7-type). In Section 2, the calculation algorithm is described. The studies of phase stability, lattice constants and site preference of PrCo7−x T x (T=Ti, Zr, Hf, Cu) are shown in Section 3. The discussion and conclusion are described in Sections 4 and 5, respectively.

In the early 1980s, Carlsson et al. initially proposed an inversion technique for obtaining the parameter-free pair potential [15]. However, the expression of their pair potential includes infinite summations, each of which contains infinite terms. This made it inconvenient for analysis. In the mid-1990s, Chen et al. proposed a concise inversion method based on the modified Möbius inversion transform in the number Theory to avoid the above shortcomings [10,11].

Based on the Chen–Möbius lattice inversion, the interatomic pair potential can be obtained from a first principle cohesive energy curves [16–19]. In general, the cohesive energy E(x) per atom in a crystal structure can be expressed as a sum of pair potential Φ(x), such that
(1)
E ( x ) =
1 2
∑
R i
≠ 0
Φ (
R i
) =
1 2
∑
n = 1
∞
r 0
( n ) Φ [
b 0
( n ) x ]
where x is the nearest-neighbor interatomic distance, R
i the lattice vector of the ith atom, r 0(n) the nth-neighbor coordination number and b 0(n)x is the nth-neighbor distance. We extend the series {b 0(n)x} to a multiplicative closed semigroup {b(n)x}, in which, for any arbitrary two integer m and n, there always exists an integer k, so that b(k)= b(m)b(n). Thus,
(2)
E ( x ) =
1 2
∑
n = 1
∞
r ( n ) Φ [ b ( n ) x ]
where
(3)
r ( n ) =
r (
b 0
− 1
[ b ( n ) ] ) ,
b ( n ) ∈ {
b 0
( n ) }
0 ,
b ( n ) ∉ {
b 0
( n ) }
Thus, the pair potential, Φ(x), can be written as:
(4)
Φ ( x ) = 2
∑
n = 1
∞
I ( n ) E [ b ( n ) x ]
where I(n), inversion coefficient, can be uniquely determined from the crystal structure as:
(5)
∑
b ( n ) | b ( k )
I ( n ) r
b
− 1
b ( k )
b ( n )

δ
k 1
Note that the I(n) is only structure dependent and is not related to concrete element category. Then the interatomic pair potentials can be obtained from the known cohesive energy function E(x), and they are used to study the rare-earth intermetallic structures.

During our calculations, cohesive energy is acquired within a first-principles framework. The total energy ab initio calculations (ESOCS 4.0 program provided by Materials Simulation Incorporation) were performed on the basis of an augmented spherical-wave (ASW) method [20,21] within the local density functional theory. The cohesive energy is obtained from:
(6)
E ( x ) =
E
tot
( x ) −
E
tot
( ∞ )
A series of total energies are calculated with various lattice constants at equal intervals of 0.1Å. In each case, for generating the total energy, more than 80 k-points in an irreducible Brillouin zone are taken into account in a self-consistent calculation. The data are then fitted by the Morse function [22], which is expressed as:
(7)
Φ ( x ) =
D 0
(
e
[ − γ ( x /
R 0
− 1 ) ]
− 2
e
[ − ( γ / 2 ) ( x /
R 0
− 1 ) ]
)
where D 0 is the depth of potential, R 0 the equilibrium distance and γ is a parameter without units. Some of the potential parameters are given in Table 1 . Several relevant interatomic pair potential curves are shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The process of evolution from ordered PrCo5 to disordered PrCo7
ce:section.ce:para: The TbCu7-type structure can be derived from the CaCu5-type structure by random substitution of a pair of Cu in 2/9 of the Ca sites [23]. In theory, considering the stochastic property of the substitution, the initial PrCo5 super-cell should be very large. For the convenience of calculation and analysis, theoretical simulation of material always uses as small super-cell as possible to approximately express the character of structural and physical properties of bulk material.

A hexagonal PrCo5 unit cell is shown in Fig. 2(1). The structure parameters of PrCo5 can be calculated by the conjugate gradient method using pair potentials Φ Co–Co(x), Φ Pr–Pr(x) and Φ Pr–Co(x) and the calculated results are a =5.017Å and c =4.066Å, which are very close to those obtained from the experiments, a =5.025Å and c =3.943Å [24].

Let the evolution process start from a (PrCo5) i × j × k super-cell, the value of i × j × k should equal to a multiple of 9. For this super-cell, 2/9 of Pr atoms are randomly selected and replaced by a pair of Co atoms mutually separated by a 0.1Å space and having random orientation. Once the pair potentials are taken into account, the orientations and the lengths of the dumbbells change immediately, and at the same time the site distribution of all the atoms is rearranged, and the lattice constants are changed (Fig. 2(2–4)). By a large quantity of testing, we find that when the initial super-cell is (PrCo5)6×2×6, the calculated XRD patterns of gained (PrCo7)56 are very similar to that derived from experiment [25] of TbCu7 structure compound (Fig. 2(5)). So (PrCo5)6×2×6 is suitable for the simulation of evolution of PrCo7 structure.

Although the PrCo7 structure is unstable in the experiment, it can be considered as the intrinsic structure of the pseudo-binary compounds of PrCo7−x T x (TbCu7-type). It is worth mentioning that, in the calculation, once the energy minimization based on the inverted atomistic pair potentials is carried out, the somehow arbitrary initial lattice structure will be stabilized to a structure close to existing TbCu7-type structure, provided that the initial model does not deviate too much from the existing TbCu7-type structure. The lattice constants of the initial model and final model are listed in Table 2 . The PrCo7 structure is also disturbed by atoms random shifted by 0.5Å. Each atom in the disturbed cell can recover its equilibrium position under the interaction of pair potentials. The randomness of the initial structure in a certain range and the stability of the final structure illustrate that PrCo7 has the topological invariance with respect to the existing TbCu7-type structure. Hence it shows that the interatomic pair potentials are reliable for studying the structural characteristics of the disordered rare-earth intermetallic compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase stability and site preference of PrCo7−x T x
ce:section.ce:para: In the process, firstly we substitute T (T=Ti, Zr, Hf, Cu) atoms for Co in each site with different concentrations. Then the energy minimization method is applied to relax the ternary system under the interaction of the pair potentials. The cohesive energy is taken as a criterion of the site preference. The results are shown in Fig. 3 . To avoid accidental errors, the results represent the arithmetic average of 30 samples. It can be seen in Fig. 3 that the system's cohesive energy decreases with the addition of the ternary element T (T=Ti, Zr, Hf). If T=Cu, the cohesive energy of PrCo7−x T x increases, which means that Cu cannot stabilize the binary PrCo7 structure. The results illustrate that each of these elements (Ti, Zr, Hf) can stabilize the binary PrCo7 structure and the corresponding stabilized phases exist. But the results of experiment indicate that PrCo7 phase cannot be stabilized by Ti and the single PrCo7−x Ti x can only be formed by mechanical milling [9]. The cohesive energy decrease most significantly when the T atoms preferentially occupy the 2e sites, less significantly when the T atoms occupy the 2c sites, and even less corresponding to the 3g sites. Therefore, the T atoms preferentially occupy the 2e sites. This is in agreement with experiments [8,9].

According to the results of the site preference, the lattice constants and volume of PrCo7−x T x are calculated when the T atoms randomly occupy the 2e sites. The calculated results are the average of 30 samples and are shown in Fig. 4 . From Fig. 4, we can find that the lattice constant a and volume show a monotonous increase with the addition of the T atoms and lattice constant c decreases firstly and then increases with T substitution. Table 3 suggests that the calculated lattice parameters are in consistent with the experimental data.

In order to check the stability of the calculated structure, we allow every atom in the PrCo7−x Zr x crystal cell to randomly move 0.5Å in arbitrary directions. Then the conjugate gradient method is used to relax this structure. The results (Table 4 ) show that the lattice constants of the final structure are the same as that of the initial structure. For the case of global deformation, the relaxed compounds can recover their initial structure too. The calculated results of PrCo6.7Zr0.3 are listed in Table 5 . Therefore, it is verified that the interatomic pair potentials are effective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular dynamic calculation for PrCo7−x Zr x
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of the calculated structure was further studied at high temperatures. The molecular dynamics NPT ensemble is applied using the method of Berendsen et al. [26] for 40,000 steps, with P =1atm, t =0.001ps. The lattice constants and potential and kinetic energies of PrCo6.86Zr0.14 and PrCo6.7Zr0.3 at temperatures of 300, 500, 700, 900 and 1100K are listed in Table 6 . It can be seen from Table 6 that the lattice constants a and c basically increase with the increase of temperature.

From Table 6, we can find that the potential and kinetic energies as well as the fluctuations all increase with the increase of temperature. Compared with the absolute potential energy values, the fluctuations are small. Therefore, the crystal structure at different temperature is basically determined by the interatomic pair potentials.

What follows is an analysis in terms of interatomic pair potential. When small quantities of the ternary element atoms T (T=Ti, Zr, Hf, Cu) substitute the Co atoms in PrCo7, the T atom is surrounded mostly by Co atoms. The Pr atoms are not their own nearest neighbor atoms, and the occasions on which T atoms are their own nearest neighbors are truly rare. Therefore, the pair potentials of Φ Pr–Pr(r) and Φ T–T(r) has less influence on the structure stability due to the substitution behavior and can be ignored. The energy difference caused by the substitution is mainly determined by the difference between Φ Co–Co(r) and Φ Co–T(r). If Φ Co–T(r)< Φ Co–Co(r), T element can stabilize the structure. On the contrary, if Φ Co–T(r)> Φ Co–Co(r), then T element cannot stabilize the structure. This can explain why the elements of Ti, Zr and Hf can stabilize the structure and Cu cannot stabilize the structure.

When the amount of ternary element atoms increases, the probabilities of T atoms are close to T atoms or to Pr atoms increase. Then the comparison between Φ Pr–Co(r)–Φ Pr–T(r) and Φ Co–Co(r)–Φ T–T(r) has to be made. We choose T=Zr as an example. Fig. 1 shows that the potential values are important in the range of 2.2Å< r <4.5Å. It is also noted that Φ Co–Co(r)< Φ Zr–Zr(r) within the range of r <3.2Å and Φ Pr–Co(r)< Φ Pr–Zr(r) with in the range of r <3.6Å. This means that substitution of a large amount of the ternary element may cause the total energy to increase thereby causing structural instability. Hence, the solubility of the ternary element will be limited.

The above analysis explains why some elements can stabilize the binary structure and some do not. Yet in the practical calculation, all of the interatomic pair potentials Φ Pr–Pr(r), Φ Pr–Co(r), Φ Pr–T(r), Φ Co–Co(r), Φ Co–T(r) and Φ T–T(r) were considered.

The site preference of the stabilizing atoms can also be simply explained by carrying out a cluster analysis of the surroundings of all the Co sites in the PrCo7−x T x crystal based on the comparison of interatomic pair potentials. Since all interatomic distances are larger than 2.2Å, and the potential values are most important when the distance is less than 4.5Å, the radius of the cluster is taken as 4.5Å. Focusing on the range of 2.2Å< r <4.5Å, one can notice that Φ Co–Co(r) intercepts with Φ Co–Zr(r) at about r =2.8Å. When the interatomic distance r <2.8Å, Φ Co–Zr(r)< Φ Co–Co(r), it is unfavorable for the substitution of Ti atoms for Co atoms. When the distance r >2.8Å, Φ Co–Zr(r)< Φ Co–Co(r), it is favorable for the substitution. On the other hand, Φ Pr–Zr(r) intercepts with Φ Pr–Co(r) at about r =3.6Å. It is unfavorable for the ternary substitution since Φ Pr–Zr(r)> Φ Pr–Co(r) at the range of r <3.6Å and favorable for the ternary substitution since at the range of 3.6Å< r <4.5Å.
Based on the above potential analysis, the site preference order for substituting T can be qualitatively estimated by accounting for the number of the benefit factors related to in Table 7 .

The first column in Table 7 includes the sites occupied by the Zr atoms. The second column shows the number of Co atoms inside a sphere centered around a Zr atom with a sphere radius of 2.8Å. The third column shows the number of Co atoms within the range of 2.8–4.5Å. Similarly, the fourth and the fifth columns correspond to center atoms being Pr. The total benefit factors are counted in the last column. When counted in the last column, whether a value from a column before should be positive or negative depends on whether the case of that column is beneficial to the energy decrease after substitution. For example, in the case of column 2 (r <2.8Å) Φ Co–Zr > Φ Co–Co when Co atoms are replaced by Zr atoms, the more Co atoms in this range, the more unfavorable for the energy decrease after substitution, so the values in this column are negative. From Table 7, we can get the conclusion that the site preference order for these stabilizing elements is 2e, 2c and 3g. This approximate estimation is in agreement with experiment.

According to our calculation of the relaxed SmCo7 structure, there are some deformations for 2c and 3g sub-lattices, which can be seen in Fig. 5 . Therefore, the local environments around every atom are different. For example, within a sphere radius of 2.8Å centered each Zr atom there may be different numbers of Co atoms. We have employed an arithmetic average of the different numbers. The results can be seen in Table 7.

The interatomic pair potentials obtained through the lattice inversion method are successfully used to explain the process of evolution from the ordered PrCo5 structure to disordered PrCo7 structure. The calculation of the phase stability, lattice parameter and site preference of PrCo7−x T x (T=Ti, Zr, Hf, Cu) are performed systematically. The results show that the cohesive energy decreases with the addition of the T (T=Ti, Zr, Hf) elements, which indicates that these elements can stabilize the structure of PrCo7−x T x . As the ternary element T substitute the Co atoms at 2e sites, the crystal total energy of PrCo7−x T x decrease most significantly. For the 2c sites substitution, the energy decrease is weak; and for the 3g sites, the energy decrease is the least. Therefore, the T atoms preferentially occupy 2e sites. The results are in agreement with the experimental data and could be explained with the pair potentials.

Despite the randomness of the initial binary PrCo7 structure and the overall structure deformation as well as random atomic motion within 0.5Å for the ternary PrCo7−x T x structure, the calculate structure supported by interatomic pair potentials can finally correspond to the existing stable PrCo7−x T x structure. The stability of the structure is further confirmed through molecular dynamic simulations at different temperatures. These facts verify that the interatomic pair potentials are effective.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported in part by the National Nature Science Foundation of China, No. 59971006, and in part by the National Advanced Materials Committee of China and Special Funds for Major State Basic Research of China, No. TG2000067101 and No. TG2000067106.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Calculated Electronic Properties of Ordered Alloys
